For founders building on AI, the constraint is moving upstream: memory and compute supply, not model quality, is increasingly what sets the ceiling on what you can ship and what it costs.

title: Anthropic and Gov. Newsom forge deal allowing California government to use Claude at half price
blurb: Anthropic struck a deal with California Governor Gavin Newsom to give every state agency and local government access to Claude at half price, along with training and support. The timing matters because the federal government has gone the other way, declaring Anthropic a "supply-chain risk" and signing with OpenAI after Anthropic refused to drop carve-outs against surveillance and autonomous weapons. Locked out of the Pentagon, Anthropic is building its public-sector beachhead at the state level instead, and California's CIO said the supply-chain designation never came up in negotiations.

title: California strikes a deal with Anthropic to expand the use of Claude products across state agencies and local governments at a 50% discount (Christine Mui/Politico)
blurb: California signed a deal with Anthropic to roll out Claude across state agencies and local governments at a 50% discount. Government is becoming a real enterprise channel for the frontier labs, with a public-sector buyer committing at scale rather than piloting. For founders selling AI into regulated or public markets, the labs are now establishing the procurement relationships and price anchors directly, which compresses the room for resellers and middleware built on top of the same models.

title: Rocket Lab buys its way into the satellite big league with $8B Iridium deal
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy satellite operator Iridium for $8B, moving from launch provider into owning an orbital constellation and a recurring connectivity business. The deal vaults it from rocket vendor to vertically integrated space company, putting it in the same league as SpaceX rather than competing for launch contracts underneath it. For a sector where launch is a thin-margin commodity, the strategic read is that the money is in owning what flies, not just getting it there.

title: Databricks’ Co-Founder Arsalan Tavakoli: Every Software Monopoly Falls in the Next 24 Months
blurb: Databricks co-founder Arsalan Tavakoli-Shiraji says any business with a monopoly today won't have one 12 to 24 months from now, as build costs, the low end, and migrations break pricing power at once. He has unusual visibility here, with Databricks running at a $6.9B revenue run-rate, growing 80%+, and AI products alone past a $1.7B run-rate. His sharper read is that enterprises are token maxing without knowing their true AI ROI, so the opening for founders is to tie AI spend to a clear business outcome rather than just standing up agents.

title: Rocket Lab continues buying spree by acquiring satellite company Iridium
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium for $54 per share, valuing it at $8B and turning the launch company into a full-stack space services player. It is at least the fifth deal in roughly a year, after Motiv, Mynaric, Geost, and a precision component maker. The prize is Iridium's in-orbit constellation and its spectrum holdings, and the move fits a broader consolidation wave that already saw Viasat take Inmarsat and Amazon buy Globalstar for $11.6B.

title: Cook Stays at Fed But Trump Wins Power Over Other Agencies
blurb: The Supreme Court let Fed Governor Lisa Cook keep her job, ruling the president can't fire a Fed governor without proof of wrongdoing. The same closely divided decision left the White House freer to remove leaders at other independent agencies, drawing a line that protects the central bank specifically rather than agency independence broadly. For founders, the practical signal is that monetary policy stays insulated from direct political pressure, so rate decisions should keep tracking the economy rather than the election calendar.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: The hidden line item in AI budgets isn't your power users, it's the high-frequency automated tasks running quietly in the background. One enterprise spent $500M on Claude in a single month because nobody set usage limits before rollout, Uber burned its entire 2026 AI budget before midyear, and JPMorgan published a report titled "AI Token Costs Are Eating into Internet Profits." The teams that avoided the surprise modeled token cost per workflow before finalizing architecture, treating cost as tokens times frequency times users rather than a function of how sophisticated the task looks.

title: Arena, the AI leaderboard everyone uses, is now a $100M business
blurb: Arena, the crowdsourced AI leaderboard built on more than 10 million user evaluations, hit $100M in annualized revenue just eight months after launching its commercial service. The money comes from AI Evaluations, a paid analytics product that sells labs and enterprises the post-training performance signal its free community generates, up from $30M when it raised a $150M Series A in January. It competes for the same post-training dollar as human-labeling shops like Mercor, Surge, and Scale, a market where appetite is surging as labs chase every last point of model performance.

title: China Births Two Robot Unicorns as Sector Funding Stays Strong
blurb: China minted two new robotics unicorns this week, each valued above $2.9B, as funding into the sector holds steady despite the broader venture slowdown. The money is flowing toward humanoid and industrial robotics players positioned to challenge Tesla and Figure AI, signaling that Beijing's hardware bet has investor conviction behind it. For founders, the takeaway is that the robotics race is now a two-front war, with Chinese capital backing domestic challengers at a pace that mirrors the US frontier.

title: Anthropic's Mythos 5 Gets US Approval for Wider Use
blurb: Anthropic regained US approval to widen access to its Mythos 5 model after resolving Trump administration national-security concerns.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture has told staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after internal meetings flagged "rapid escalation in AI token spend." The shift to consumption-based pricing, combined with employees "tokenmaxxing" to prove their AI uptake, has pushed spend to an inflection point where C-level execs are openly questioning the return. Accenture's agentic AI lead says this isn't niche, every AI-using enterprise will hit it, which is why the consultancy now plans to sell clients the service of untangling their own token bills.

title: In Q2, there were thirteen $1B+ US venture-backed startup exits, either through acquisition or IPO, the most exits since the 2021 market peak (Joanna Glasner/Crunchbase News)
blurb: US venture-backed startups produced thirteen $1B+ exits in Q2 through acquisition or IPO, the most in a single quarter since the 2021 market peak. The activity signals that the long exit drought, which left more than 1,500 unicorns with nowhere to go, is finally starting to thaw at the top end. For founders and their backers, large liquidity events returning means the capital locked in late-stage paper has a clearer path back to LPs, which tends to loosen funding further down the stack.

title: SCOTUS finds that Trump's 2025 firing of FTC Commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter without cause was lawful, placing independence of several agencies in doubt (NPR)
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled that Trump's 2025 dismissal of FTC Commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter without cause was lawful, eroding the for-cause protections that have shielded independent agencies since 1935. The decision puts the FTC, FCC, SEC, and NLRB under tighter White House control, meaning antitrust, securities, and labor enforcement can now swing with each administration. For founders, the regulators who police mergers, fundraising disclosures, and hiring practices are becoming political instruments, so the rules you build around today may not survive the next election cycle.

title: Comcast Plans to Spin Off NBCUniversal Into New Media Firm
blurb: Comcast plans to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate publicly traded media company, splitting its content businesses from cable-TV and internet. The move follows the same logic playing out across legacy media, where shrinking linear-TV assets get carved off so the faster-growing connectivity business can be valued on its own terms. For founders, it is another signal that the bundle era is unwinding and conglomerates are betting that two focused companies are worth more than one diversified giant.

title: SpaceX's $25 billion bond sale drives huge demand - and a potential headache for investors
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in senior unsecured notes last week, pulling nearly $90B in orders just two weeks after the largest IPO in history. The catch is that owning both SPCX equity and SpaceX bonds isn't diversification, it's the same execution risk priced into two instruments, with both the equity story and the debt service riding on Starlink scaling and Starship working. Equity investors took the hint, selling the stock down more than 13% on the week against a $5B net loss and capex that more than doubled year over year.

title: A former Tesla Optimus engineer settled a trade secret lawsuit and raised $11M to build the robot hands his old employer still cannot crack
blurb: A former Tesla Optimus electronics lead, Jay Li, settled Tesla's trade secret suit and raised an $11M seed led by First Round Capital to ship dexterous robotic hands from his startup Proception. Tesla had accused Li of downloading robotic-hand actuation files before resigning and founding the company six days later, but the case was dismissed this month and Proception is now shipping its first hands to researchers and humanoid makers. Dexterous manipulation remains one of robotics' hardest unsolved problems, and Li bets his data-collection approach gets there faster than the decade some researchers predict.

title: AWS raises GPU Capacity Block prices again as AI demand outstrips supply
blurb: AWS is raising prices on its EC2 Capacity Blocks GPU reservation service by around 20%, the second hike this year after a 15% jump in January. An H200-based p5e.48xlarge instance that ran $34.61 per hour before January will cost about $39 in most regions and $49.74 in U.S. West after July 1. Hyperscalers have locked up the bulk of Nvidia production through multibillion-dollar forward orders, so enterprise buyers face longer lead times and rising bills, with compute costs likely to keep climbing as the memory shortage drags on.

title: AirTrunk Is Said to Be Near Confidential Filing for Biggest Singapore IPO in Years
blurb: Blackstone-backed AirTrunk is close to a confidential filing for a Singapore REIT IPO that would be the city-state's largest in years. The listing turns a privately held hyperscale data center operator into public infrastructure stock, giving investors a direct way to buy the physical buildout behind the AI compute boom. For founders, it is another sign that the capital crowding into AI has moved past models and chips into the power, land, and real estate that everything else depends on.

title: Accel backs Pocket’s $11M raise, as the AI hardware startup turns a viral launch into $27M ARR
blurb: Pocket, an AI device that records and summarizes conversations, raised $11M led by Accel after turning a 2024 viral concept video into $27M in annualized revenue. The startup has shipped more than 35,000 units since its October 2025 launch, with monthly growth topping 50% in some months and enterprise customers like DoorDash on board. That traction stands out in a hardware category littered with high-profile flops, and the difference is a narrow, opt-in use case with HIPAA and SOC 2 compliance rather than an always-on ambient gadget chasing a vague promise.

title: Straiker, which develops tech for securing enterprise AI agents, raised a $64M Series A, bringing its total funding to $85M (Chris Metinko/Axios)
blurb: Straiker raised a $64M Series A to secure enterprise AI agents, pushing its total funding to $85M. The round signals that as companies hand real tasks to autonomous agents, the security layer for them is becoming its own venture category rather than a feature inside existing tools. For founders deploying agents in production, the gap between what an agent can do and what it can be trusted to do is now where the money is going.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that an AI bubble could burst and drag the broader economy down with it, citing the concentration of market gains and capex in a handful of tech firms. The central bankers' bank flagged that AI-driven equity valuations and surging data-center spending now sit at the center of global growth, so a correction would not stay contained to tech. For founders, the read is that a downturn in AI sentiment would tighten capital and demand well beyond the labs, even for companies with no direct AI exposure.

title: UK founders signed the most investor-friendly terms in 5 years in 2025 and most of them didn’t know it
blurb: UK VC investment jumped to £11.2 billion across 711 term sheets in 2025, up from £7.6 billion in 2024, but HSBC Innovation Banking found the terms behind those deals quietly tilted toward investors. Founders signed the most investor-friendly conditions in five years, with the control provisions, liquidation preferences, and downside protections buried in the fine print rather than the headline valuation. The lesson for founders is that a hot funding year masks tougher terms, and the number that matters most is rarely the one in the press release.

title: Robot hand company settles Tesla trade secret suit and announces $11M raise
blurb: Proception, founded by a former Tesla Optimus technical lead, settled the trade-secret suit Tesla filed against it and raised an $11M seed led by First Round Capital, with Y Combinator and BoxGroup joining. The company is now shipping its first high-dexterity robotic hands to researchers and robotics companies, betting that most humanoid builders would rather buy dexterous manipulation than develop it. That bet sits against a hard consensus, with academics estimating human-equivalent robot hands are still a decade out, even as capital floods the rest of the robotics stack.

title: Alibaba, Tencent Dropped by DC Lobbyists to Comply With US Curbs
blurb: Washington's top lobbying firms are dropping Chinese tech clients like Alibaba and Tencent to comply with new US restrictions. The curbs are reshaping the influence game, cutting off the access these companies relied on to shape policy and defend their US operations. For any startup with cross-border ambitions, the signal is that the regulatory wall between US and Chinese tech is hardening, and the intermediaries who used to smooth it over are walking away.

title: From charging EVs and drones to powering data centres: London-based Gaussion raises €24.5 million
blurb: Gaussion, a UCL spinout, raised €24.5M co-led by BGF and AlbionVC to scale its magnetic charging tech that speeds up battery charging and extends pack life without touching the underlying chemistry. The system retrofits onto any lithium-ion battery and spans automotive, aerospace, grid storage, and data centre power, with 66 patent applications across 17 families already filed. The bet is that the battery, not the chip, becomes the binding constraint on AI compute, drones, and EVs, and the companies that move the physics ceiling capture the value others assume sits in silicon.

title: Amid rising UK cyber talent growth, Osney Capital closes oversubscribed €69 million debut fund
blurb: Specialist capital keeps winning in a tight fundraising market. Osney Capital closed its debut fund oversubscribed at its €69M hard cap, backed by the British Business Bank, Imperial College London's endowment, and accreditation from the UK's national security investment fund, making it one of the country's first dedicated cybersecurity Seed funds. The thesis is sovereign cyber capability, as geopolitical tension pushes governments and industry toward home-grown defense. Osney has already written seven pre-Seed and Seed checks between €289k and €2.8M, against a target of 30 companies.

title: Quantifind, whose AI products help banks combat financial crimes such as money laundering, raised $200M led by Summit Partners (Laura Kreutzer/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Quantifind, whose AI helps banks detect money laundering and financial crime, raised $200M led by Summit Partners.

title: Omen AI raises $31m to watch the water inside AI data centres
blurb: Omen AI raised a $31M Series A, led by Nava Ventures, to monitor the fluid inside liquid-cooled AI data centres. As operators push more water through hotter racks to absorb heat, the mix invites bacteria that clogs flow, and the fix is a flush that can pull a rack offline for five or six hours at a cost in the millions. Omen sells a continuous spectrometer that flags chemical trouble before it forces that downtime, betting that cooling is the unglamorous bottleneck as compute scales.

title: Drag reduction aerospace startup Mako takes off with $28 million Series A
blurb: Mako, the Sydney startup behind a shark skin-inspired film that cuts aircraft drag, raised a $28M Series A led by Virescent Ventures, with IAG, Grok Ventures, and Skip Capital joining. The Flightfilm product reduces drag and fuel burn by roughly 4% per aircraft, tested on a US Air Force C-130J, with Delta, Vueling, and Asia Pacific carriers lined up for trials. The funding goes toward certification in Australia, Europe, and the US, plus roll-to-roll manufacturing for pre-orders as airlines hunt efficiency gains against a fuel cost shock.

title: Mozilla warns of indirect prompt injection risk in AI coding agents
blurb: Mozilla's 0DIN team showed that a malicious GitHub repo can compromise a developer's machine via indirect prompt injection, with no malicious code in the repo itself. The proof-of-concept tricks agents like Claude Code into running setup steps that pipe an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record to bash, fetching a reverse shell at runtime that's invisible to code review, static analysis, and the agent reading the repo.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a proof of concept for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation bug that lets any local user gain root by corrupting file-backed page-cache memory through zero-copy socket buffer paths. The fix landed in v7.1-rc5, but only kernels carrying the full DirtyFrag patch chain are protected, leaving Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu systems with unprivileged user namespaces exposed.

title: Pollen tried to remove my article and Google is assisting with it
blurb: The Pragmatic Engineer's Gergely Orosz reports that Google delisted his 2022 article on events startup Pollen's $800M collapse after receiving a bogus copyright claim, despite him being the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub critical coverage from search results, with Google honoring the takedown by default and leaving the author to appeal.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: Git 2.55.0 adds a `git history fixup <commit-id>` subcommand that stages and amends changes into an existing commit in one step, automatically rebasing any stacked branches that contain it instead of requiring an interactive autosquash rebase. The release also extends the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This reference shows how Linkerd's sidecar proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request the moment a workload is meshed, with no instrumentation, SDK calls, or image rebuilds, giving you the east-west traffic that application-layer OpenTelemetry never sees. It walks through where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, then wires the proxy's Prometheus endpoint into an existing OTel Collector pipeline so both layers land in the same backend.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found real-world RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros in their moduli, a sparsity pattern that makes them far easier to factor, surfaced by scanning Certificate Transparency logs, TLS/SSH scans, and PGP dumps with the open-source badkeys project. The flaw traces to independent implementations failing the same way, including CompleteFTP's key generation and certificates once issued to Yahoo and Verizon, suggesting cryptanalytic attacks tuned for this failure class are worth building.

title: The attack that hijacked Claude Code came through Sentry. Datadog, PagerDuty, and Jira have the same exposure.
blurb: Tenet Security's "agentjacking" disclosure shows that a single crafted Sentry error event, sent through a public DSN that needs no authentication, can inject attacker instructions that Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute as trusted diagnostic output with the developer's full privileges. Tenet hit an 85% success rate across 100-plus controlled targets, found 2,388 organizations with exposed DSNs, and warns the same exposure applies to any MCP-connected source like Datadog, PagerDuty, or Jira.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" ships from 2842 contributors and 59703 commits, updating Nixpkgs with 20442 new packages and bumping GNOME to 50 and GCC to 15. Stage 1 initrd now defaults to systemd with the scripted implementation deprecated for removal in 26.11, and this is the final release to support x86_64-darwin following Apple's platform deprecation.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell for SSH, where each app is a minimal HTTP server exposed over a Unix domain socket rather than a localhost port, with encryption handled at the SSH layer instead of in the app. The shell provides a home screen of apps plus an API for inter-app lookups, so a text-editor app can register itself and open files double-clicked elsewhere.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iOS app, bringing its agentic coding tools to iPhone and iPad following SpaceX's acquisition of the company earlier this month. The app launches cloud coding agents from anywhere, supports voice conversations, diff inspection, and PR merges, with Remote Control handoffs between local and cloud agents planned next and 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
blurb: Mozilla's 0Din researchers demonstrated an attack where a clean-looking repository hijacks a developer's machine through Claude Code, with no malicious code in the repo itself. A deliberate install error tells the agent to run `axiom init`, which executes a script that pulls a base64-encoded reverse shell from a DNS TXT record, splitting the payload across three systems no single tool inspects together.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by `git repack --write-midx=incremental`, which writes incremental multi-pack index chains directly instead of rewriting a single MIDX over every pack. The append-only mode lets large repositories index newly created packs by adding a layer to the chain, avoiding the expensive full-file rewrite that single-MIDX maintenance forces on each small update.

title: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA can be bypassed with a stock photo
blurb: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA, which extracts 21 hand-knuckle coordinates from camera video, can be passed by feeding a stock image through OBS Virtual Camera.

title: WATaBoy: JIT-Ing Game Boy Instructions to WASM Beats a Native Interpreter
blurb: A developer building a Game Boy emulator for their final-year project generated WebAssembly bytecode from Game Boy instructions, exploiting the JIT compilation that JavaScriptCore applies to hot Wasm in browsers, the one path around Apple's iOS JIT ban. The JIT-to-Wasm approach stays cycle-accurate by predicting interrupts and falling back to an interpreter, and it beats a natively running interpreter on performance.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel?
blurb: This post traces a single CUDA vector-add kernel from source down to the warps that execute it and back, covering how nvcc fans out to multiple compilers (cicc, ptxas, fatbinary) to produce PTX, SASS, and an embedded fatbin. The walkthrough then follows the launch through the driver stack, accounting for the tens of millions of CPU instructions, two device files, ~900 ioctls, and one memory-mapped doorbell register that turn a million float additions into a result.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: The author argues Qwen 3.6 27B is the first local model practical enough to serve as a general-purpose coding assistant, citing a hexagonal minesweeper it built correctly from a single prompt with proper pnpm packaging. The dense 27B variant runs locally via llama.cpp at Q8_0 quantization and outperforms the faster 35B A3B mixture-of-experts version on instruction-following, at the cost of speed and a hot machine.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is a terminal-based multiplexer for managing multiple coding agents, using workspaces, tabs, and panes that you control with the mouse or tmux-style keybindings. Agents keep running after you detach and reattach, and unlike Electron or native GUI wrappers, you interact with each agent's real terminal process rather than a rewritten view of it.

title: Deno update streamlines creation of desktop apps
blurb: Deno Land released Deno 2.9 with an experimental deno desktop feature that compiles a script or web framework project into a single self-contained binary, running the UI in a webview and the logic in Deno via the same machinery as deno compile. The release also cuts hello-world cold start from 34ms to 17ms, holds resident memory flat near 62MB under load, and advances Node.js compatibility to version 26.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: The author reverse-engineers ASIF, the sparse disk image format Apple introduced in macOS Tahoe for its Virtualization framework, walking through the process of writing a parser from scratch. The post starts with eyeballing hexdumps and a numbered test pattern, then decodes the file magic and big-endian header fields, with research notes throughout aimed at readers new to reverse-engineering binary formats.

title: Cursor now has a mobile app for guiding your coding agent on the go
blurb: Cursor released an iOS app that lets developers spin up new coding agents or steer ones started from the desktop client, extending the agent-oriented workflow introduced in Cursor 2.0. It follows comparable mobile apps from Anthropic and OpenAI, reflecting a shift toward overseeing code-writing agents from a phone rather than editing large codebases on a multi-monitor desktop setup.

title: Pong game recompiles its own source code every frame — winning entry at IOCCC29 was generated by a custom compiler
blurb: An IOCCC29-winning Pong recompiles its own source every frame, each run printing the C for the next frame with state baked in.

title: Visual Studio Code locks down untrusted code
blurb: VS Code 1.26 ships Workspace Trust, which now opens unfamiliar project folders in Restricted Mode to block automatic code execution until the developer explicitly trusts the folder. The release also surfaces total cost across an entire chat session rather than per-turn, and lets a single Copilot agent host run multiple chats side-by-side that share the same working context.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: The GitHub Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, five times its typical output and still not enough as private vulnerability reports climbed from ~550 to over 3,000 per week. GitHub argues review times will stay longer until submitters carry more of the load by filing complete data and requesting CVEs only when they intend to publish.

title: CachyOS June 2026 Release
blurb: CachyOS June 2026 release adds a Hyprland Noctalia desktop, DNS-over-QUIC via blocky, extended Python PGO, and GCC branch-misprediction tuning.

title: Why Playwright MCP Cost Us 5 More Tokens Than We Expected
blurb: The author benchmarked the full browser-automation workflow and found that Playwright MCP's ephemeral element references (button [ref=e5]) only survive a single snapshot, so generating reusable Playwright scripts forces the model to re-observe the page, re-parse the accessibility tree, and retry selectors. That iteration, not observation quality, drove cost to 3,211 tokens and roughly $0.04 per generated script, which their tool Brocogni cuts by precomputing ranked selectors before the LLM sees the page.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: This post maps the CSS pseudo-classes that behave like JavaScript event listeners, including :hover, :active, :focus, :focus-visible, and :focus-within, explaining why each tracks state rather than firing on events. It then walks through the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly and drive animations without JavaScript.

title: Synthesize the big picture and analyze trends with BigQuery's AI.AGG function
blurb: BigQuery's new AI.AGG() function, now in preview, lets you summarize or synthesize across millions of rows of unstructured or multimodal data using natural-language instructions inside a single line of SQL. It targets aggregate questions that per-row AI functions can't answer, like surfacing the top feature requests across negative reviews or clustering Spark logs to find memory thrashing and clock drift before anything throws a FATAL error.

title: Scaling Network Analysis for Fraud Prevention with BigQuery Graph
blurb: Curve, a UK financial super-app, moved its fraud network analysis to BigQuery Graph, using native GQL to run multi-hop queries across millions of users and tens of millions of connections without standing up a separate graph database. Keeping the data in BigQuery avoided a migration to a dedicated graph store, cutting cost while surfacing the shared-device and shared-card links that organized fraud rings use to evade per-transaction models.

title: No, you haven't accidentally downloaded a banking app – that really is the new Winter Olympics 2030 logo
blurb: Alpes 2030 revealed the Winter Olympics identity, a mark of radiant lines converging into an alpine peak rendered in ice-blue and alpenglow pink gradients, with the Paralympic version inverting the same shape into a connected system. The execution is clean and scales from venue signage to avatars, though its geometric minimalism reads closer to a fintech brand than a winter sports event.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: CSS keeps absorbing behavior that once required JavaScript event listeners, and this walkthrough maps the pseudo-classes that now track interaction states: :hover, :active, :focus-visible, :focus-within, and :has(). It also covers the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly, with a useful clarification that pseudo-classes match states rather than events even when they feel like listeners.

title: A Teatro and Balcony Studio tuck Museum Tower House into steep site in Los Angeles
blurb: A Teatro and Balcony Studio completed Museum Tower House, a slender vertical residence wedged into a steep hillside lot in LA's Mt Washington neighborhood. The facades use striated plaster, and interior rooms are sequenced to unfold like museum galleries, with a two-car garage anchoring the single-family home to its grade.

title: Apple just acquired the app that won last year’s ‘Innovation’ Apple Design Award
blurb: Apple acquired Play, the SwiftUI prototyping tool that won the 2025 Apple Design Award for Innovation, with the app pulled from the App Store and its team offered employment at Apple. The likely path is integration into Xcode or Apple's free developer resources, narrowing the gap between design prototyping and shipped SwiftUI code while removing a standalone tool designers had relied on.

title: Mozilla warns of indirect prompt injection risk in AI coding agents
blurb: Mozilla's 0DIN team demonstrated an indirect prompt injection attack in which a malicious GitHub repo steers AI coding agents like Claude Code into running setup commands that fetch a reverse-shell payload at runtime via a DNS TXT record, leaving nothing malicious in the repo for code review or static analysis to catch. Treat setup instructions in unfamiliar repos as untrusted code regardless of agent recommendations, and design agents to surface what a command actually executes rather than its literal string.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a PoC for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation flaw in the networking stack's socket-buffer handling that lets any local user gain root. The bug is a variant of the DirtyFrag/Fragnesia family affecting Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu with unprivileged user namespaces enabled; only kernels updated to v7.1-rc5 with the complete fix chain are protected, so partially patched LTS branches remain exposed.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers analyzing badkeys data found a class of weak RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros, including certificates issued to Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP (RSA keys from v10.0.0-12.0.0, DSA keys from v10.0.0-23.0.4). The sparse moduli are factorable, and the recurrence of the same failure across independent implementations suggests more vulnerable products remain undiscovered and worth scanning for.

title: Critical SimpleHelp flaw exploited to deploy new stealer malware
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication-bypass in SimpleHelp's OIDC configuration that lets unauthenticated users create privileged technician accounts, to push the TaskWeaver loader and the previously undocumented Djinn Stealer across Windows, macOS, and Linux. Roughly 1,000 internet-exposed SimpleHelp servers ran the vulnerable config at disclosure, so MSPs and IT teams running RMM on the edge should patch immediately and rotate developer, cloud, Git, SSH, and infrastructure credentials.

title: US Offers $10 Million Bounty for Russian State Hackers as Messaging App Attacks Evolve
blurb: The US State Department is offering up to $10M for information on UNC5792 and UNC4221, Russian intelligence-linked actors phishing officials, military leaders, and Ukrainian targets to hijack Signal and WhatsApp accounts. A fresh CISA/FBI update warns the actors now request victims' Backup Recovery Keys, which stay valid across re-registration on the same number, so compromised users must generate a new key to revoke access.

title: Hackers now exploit critical Oracle E-Business flaw in attacks
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), an unauthenticated, low-complexity RCE in the File Transmission component of Oracle E-Business Suite's Payments product that allows full system takeover over HTTP. Oracle patched it in its May 2026 Critical Patch Update, but Shadowserver tracks over 450 exposed EBS instances, so operators should apply the fix immediately and hunt for compromise.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it tracks as StegoAd, which used steganography to hide payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 image and font files, then activated days post-install to steal credentials and run ad fraud. Active since at least 2021, the extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, using server-side validation, DevTools detection, and execution gates to evade analysis.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review
blurb: OpenAI and Anthropic restricted their newest AI models to government-approved customers during a Trump administration cybersecurity review, with OpenAI limiting GPT-5.6 Sol to vetted partners and Anthropic redeploying its Mythos 5 model to a small group of cyber defenders after a Commerce Department ban. The vetting follows a June executive order letting the government assess national-security risks of frontier models for up to 30 days before public release.

title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
blurb: Mozilla's 0Din researchers demonstrated an indirect prompt-injection attack against Claude Code in which a benign-looking repository's setup notes trigger an install error whose message instructs the agent to run a recovery command that executes setup.sh, which fetches a base64-encoded payload from a DNS TXT record and spawns a reverse shell. The payload never lives in the repo and leaves no plaintext reverse-shell signature, so static analysis, network monitoring, and the agent each see only benign activity while attackers exfiltrate credentials and deploy persistent backdoors.

title: Insurance Regulators Group NAIC Hit in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
blurb: NAIC confirmed attackers exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day (CVE-2026-35273, unauthenticated RCE) to access its systems on June 11, exfiltrating statutory financial reporting data, credit rating agency records, and outdated logs and configuration files, though it says PII and payment data were untouched. ShinyHunters is behind the broader campaign against 100-plus organizations and listed NAIC on its leak site, making the insurance regulator the first victim to publicly confirm compromised data.

title: U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users
blurb: The U.S. State Department is offering up to $10M through its Rewards for Justice program for information identifying members of UNC5792 and UNC4221, two groups tied to Russia's FSB and military intelligence. The actors run phishing campaigns against Signal and WhatsApp accounts of U.S. and NATO officials, impersonating Signal support to steal backup recovery keys and compromising thousands of accounts without breaking the platforms' encryption.

title: GPT-5.6 gets better at cybersecurity
blurb: OpenAI began rolling out its GPT-5.6 model series (Sol, Terra, and Luna) in limited preview to trusted partners via API and Codex, coordinating the release with the U.S. government before wider availability. Sol advances long-horizon security tasks like vulnerability research and exploitation but could not complete a full cyberattack autonomously in OpenAI's tests, which paired the model with a layered safety stack that refuses disguised offensive cyber and biology requests.

title: JSP webshells being dropped on unpatched PTC Windchill instances
blurb: CVE-2026-12569, an improper input validation flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM enabling unauthenticated remote code execution, is being exploited in the wild to drop JSP webshells, and CISA has added it to its KEV catalog. PTC shipped a patch on June 18 and is updating its advisory with indicators of compromise, so organizations running either PLM platform should patch and hunt for IOCs.

title: The attack that hijacked Claude Code came through Sentry. Datadog, PagerDuty, and Jira have the same exposure.
blurb: Tenet Security's "agentjacking" disclosure shows a single crafted Sentry error event, sent via a public DSN that needs no authentication, can inject attacker instructions that Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute with developer privileges, succeeding in 85% of 100-plus controlled tests against the 2,388 orgs with exposed DSNs. The same exposure hits any agent pulling from Datadog, PagerDuty, or Jira, so the fix is restricting what shell-capable agents do with returned data, not revoking the intentionally public DSN.

title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
blurb: Mustang Panda is running two espionage campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets, deploying SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and the novel ZOHOMURK implant, which abuses hardcoded Zoho WorkDrive OAuth credentials to use an attacker-controlled cloud account as a command-and-control dead drop that blends with normal traffic. Acronis confirmed active compromises of senior administrative machines, attributes the activity with high confidence based on reused Toneshell code and shared infrastructure, and worked with CERT-In on cleanup.

title: Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse
blurb: ESET reported that Russian APT Gamaredon ran 35 spear-phishing campaigns against Ukrainian government and military targets in 2025, deploying six new PowerShell tools and weaponizing a patched WinRAR flaw (CVE-2025-8088) to drop HTA downloaders into the Startup folder for persistence. The group increasingly routed command-and-control through tunnel services and serverless worker platforms to mask its back-end infrastructure, so defenders should monitor outbound traffic to these providers and patch WinRAR.

title: 236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers
blurb: Infoblox identified 236,493 distinct second-level domains running investment-scam templates built on DCloud Uni-App, a legitimate Chinese cross-platform framework, powering fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering operations, WhatsApp phishing, rigged gambling sites, and wallet drainers since mid-2022. Technical fingerprints, victim-communication patterns, and bulletproof hosting suggest centralized ownership across a large share of the sites, including the RainbowEx Ponzi scheme that defrauded tens of thousands in Argentina.

title: Nissan says Oracle PeopleSoft break-in may have spilled payroll records, SSNs
blurb: Nissan disclosed that attackers breached its Oracle PeopleSoft environment and may have exfiltrated payroll records and Social Security numbers belonging to current and former employees. The carmaker has not confirmed how the intrusion occurred, but the timing aligns with the wave of Oracle E-Business and PeopleSoft attacks tied to Cl0p, so organizations running internet-facing PeopleSoft should audit access logs and assume data exposure.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS and iPadOS 26.5.2 and macOS 26.5.2, patching nearly 30 security issues that center on WebKit and WebRTC, with additional kernel-level fixes. The flaws were first addressed in the iOS 26.6 betas; Apple recommends all iPhone, iPad, and Mac users install the update, since web-engine bugs are common drive-by exploitation vectors.

title: In major privacy win, Supreme Court rules geofence warrants are protected by privacy rights
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that geofence warrants fall under Fourth Amendment protection, holding that individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy in their cell-phone location data and that police must obtain a search warrant to compel companies like Google to hand it over. The court declined to ban geofence warrants outright, instead requiring law enforcement to narrow these "reverse" search requests rather than sweeping up the location records of every nearby user.

title: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA can be bypassed with a stock photo
blurb: Google's experimental hand-gesture reCAPTCHA, which asks users to wave at their camera and extracts 21 hand-knuckle coordinates via AI to verify humanness, can be defeated with a static stock photo of a hand fed through OBS Virtual Camera. Researchers reproduced the bypass across multiple still images and note it requires no AI bot or video, so a simple Python script can automate it end to end.

title: The Bear Necessities: A Look at the Drivers, Dynamics, and Applications of the Pro-Russia Influence Ecosystem
blurb: Google Threat Intelligence Group reports that the pro-Russia influence ecosystem has matured from a wartime tool into a global strategic asset, pivoting from Ukraine-specific goals back toward the EU, NATO, and other long-standing Kremlin targets. The group expects influence operations and hacktivism to intensify outside Ukraine, increasingly powered by generative AI for planning, research, and content creation. Defenders should treat the ecosystem as resilient to narrow takedowns, since its interconnected components survive limited-scope disruption.

title: SCOTUS limits the law enforcement use of "geofence" warrants, saying people have "a reasonable expectation of privacy" in their cell-phone location data (TechCrunch)
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled that geofence warrants, which compel providers to identify every device in a geographic area during a time window, violate the Fourth Amendment because users hold a reasonable expectation of privacy in their cell-phone location data. The decision curtails a technique law enforcement has used to canvass suspects from location histories, and agencies relying on bulk geolocation requests will need narrower, particularized warrants going forward.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that collecting phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending the Carpenter precedent to geofence warrants. The decision means law enforcement requests for bulk location data from providers like Google now face Fourth Amendment scrutiny, though the court declined to rule on whether Chatrie's specific warrant was valid.

title: Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027
blurb: Microsoft extended hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, one year past the original October 2026 end date. The extension applies only to systems enrolled in hotpatch updates, which receive monthly security patches by modifying in-memory process code without requiring a restart, reducing reboot-driven downtime during vulnerability response.

title: Microsoft Edge gets tons of security features, including AI model that can see your screen
blurb: Microsoft detailed new Edge for Business security controls, including DLP policies that block copy-paste and downloads from unmanaged locations, contractor file isolation to OneDrive, extension installation governance, and native Defender and Purview integration. Edge also added an on-device computer-vision model that watches the screen to block scareware and malicious content in real time without relying on site reputation.

title: FCC escalates China crackdown by closing equipment import loophole
blurb: The FCC moved to bar imports of Chinese-made network equipment that received authorization before 2022, closing a loophole the agency says could have let Covered List vendors like Huawei and ZTE maintain access to U.S. critical infrastructure. The rules take effect in July and extend Chairman Brendan Carr's China crackdown, which already covers foreign-made routers, blanket carrier authorizations, and subsea cable supply chains following the Salt Typhoon intrusions.

title: Microsoft to assist European Commission in defense of EU-US data-sharing agreement
blurb: Microsoft agreed to support the European Commission's legal defense of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the transatlantic agreement that lets companies move Europeans' personal data to US servers under approved safeguards. A court strike-down would invalidate the legal basis many cloud and SaaS providers rely on for EU-to-US transfers, forcing security and compliance teams back to standard contractual clauses and fresh transfer-risk assessments.

title: Private keys, not smart contracts, caused 40% of crypto's $16 billion hack losses. Here's whats being done.
blurb: Roughly 40% of crypto's $16.69B in cumulative hack losses trace to stolen private keys rather than smart-contract or blockchain flaws, according to DeFiLlama data cited by CoinDesk. Operational and key-management failures across people, systems, and third-party tools now outpace contract exploits as attackers shift to the weakest link. Defenders are moving to multi-party computation and account abstraction to eliminate single-key control and raise the cost of compromise.

title: What the post-quantum executive order really demands of CISOs
blurb: A new executive order sets binding post-quantum deadlines: federal high-value systems must move key establishment to PQC by Dec. 31, 2030, and digital signatures by Dec. 31, 2031. The author argues CISOs should treat this as a readiness and ownership problem, not a future compliance checkbox, since "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection of long-lived data is already operational and procurement and migration cycles put 2030 inside current planning horizons.

title: US seizes hundreds of FIFA World Cup illegal streaming domains
blurb: The DOJ Criminal Division seized nearly 400 domains streaming 2026 FIFA World Cup matches without authorization, in a coordinated takedown dubbed Operation Offsides spanning Peru, Bulgaria, Croatia, Romania, Poland, and Colombia. Beyond copyright enforcement, officials flagged that illegal sports-streaming sites routinely expose visitors to malware and credential theft, a vector worth noting as the tournament drives traffic to lookalike and pirate domains.

title: Robot Police Officers
blurb: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office deployed a drone equipped with a high-powered magnet to disarm a barricaded suspect, using it to pull a knife from the person's hand inside a residence after negotiations failed. The June 22 incident marks an early operational use of remote robotics for direct physical intervention in police standoffs, raising open questions about use-of-force policy and oversight as the tactic spreads.

title: 'Chinese Mythos' adds further fuel to sovereignty debate in AI security
blurb: Qihoo 360, the Chinese firm placed on the U.S. Entity List in 2020, claimed to have built Tulongfeng and Yitianzhen, with CEO Hongyi Zhou pitching the latter as "China's version of Mythos," Anthropic's automated vulnerability researcher. Zhou said Tulongfeng has surfaced 3,432 flaws across open-source, binary, and agentic systems, though the figures are unverified and trail Mythos partners' 10,000-plus confirmed findings under Project Glasswing.

title: Crypto analytics firm Chainalysis proposes standards for blockchain tracing
blurb: Chainalysis published a proposed ontology for blockchain tracing on Monday, defining how investigators cluster crypto addresses and gauge their confidence in linking them to a single entity. The standard draws on the firm's Reactor tool, which survived a Daubert hearing in the 2024 Bitcoin Fog money-laundering conviction, and aims to give law enforcement a defensible methodology for evidence in court.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: GitHub's Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, more than 5x its typical monthly output and the highest on record. Private vulnerability reports jumped from ~550/week in January to over 3,000/week, and GitHub CNA CVE requests hit nearly 4,000 in May, roughly 10x year-over-year. Review times are now longer, but advisories remain human-validated. Defenders can help by submitting complete vulnerability data, coordinating with maintainers, and requesting CVEs only with clear intent to publish.

title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
blurb: WhatsApp began global rollout of optional usernames, letting its 3B+ users be contacted without sharing phone numbers and hiding numbers from other accounts entirely. Users can also set a username key, a second secret required for first contact that can be reset to cut off inbound messages, arriving more than two years after Signal shipped the same protection.

title: Straiker, which develops tech for securing enterprise AI agents, raised a $64M Series A, bringing its total funding to $85M (Chris Metinko/Axios)
blurb: Straiker, which builds security tooling for enterprise AI agents, raised a $64M Series A, bringing its total funding to $85M. The round reflects rising investment in agentic-AI security as enterprises deploy autonomous agents that expand the attack surface through tool access, prompt injection, and unscoped permissions.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it calls StegoAd, which hid payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 files using steganography to evade scanners before stealing credentials and running ad fraud. The campaign had been live since 2021 across a 2.6 million install base, exposing how thinly the Edge Add-ons store vets the ad blockers, VPNs, and translators enterprise users sideload onto managed browsers.

title: U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users
blurb: The U.S. State Department is offering up to $10 million through its Rewards for Justice program for information identifying members of UNC5792 and UNC4221, two Russian state-linked groups that phished Signal and WhatsApp accounts of U.S. and NATO officials. The campaigns compromised thousands of accounts by impersonating Signal support agents to steal backup recovery keys, not by breaking the apps' encryption.

title: Hackers now exploit critical Oracle E-Business flaw in attacks
blurb: Attackers have begun actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817, a critical unauthenticated takeover flaw (CVSS 9.8) in Oracle E-Business Suite's Payments component, with the first attempts spotted over the weekend and over 450 EBS instances still exposed online. Oracle patched the bug in its May 2026 Critical Patch Update, so the risk falls on enterprises that delayed applying it, following the Clop gang's earlier mass exploitation of a separate EBS flaw.

title: Critical SimpleHelp flaw exploited to deploy new stealer malware
blurb: Hackers are exploiting a critical authentication-bypass flaw in SimpleHelp (CVE-2026-48558) to create privileged technician accounts and deploy two previously undocumented tools, the TaskWeaver loader and Djinn Stealer, an cross-platform infostealer focused on developer and cloud credentials. Because SimpleHelp is RMM software used by MSPs and IT helpdesks, a single compromised server hands attackers a trusted admin channel into every managed endpoint, and roughly 1,000 exposed servers were running the vulnerable OIDC configuration at disclosure.

title: Insurance Regulators Group NAIC Hit in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
blurb: The NAIC confirmed attackers exploited Oracle's PeopleSoft zero-day CVE-2026-35273 to access its systems on June 11, making the insurance regulator the first organization to publicly confirm data compromise in the ShinyHunters campaign. NAIC says exposed data was limited to public statutory filings, credit-rating data, and outdated logs, with no PII or financial account information taken, though ShinyHunters claims it stole 260,000 insurer regulatory documents.

title: JSP webshells being dropped on unpatched PTC Windchill instances
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-12569, an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in PTC's Windchill and FlexPLM product lifecycle management platforms, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after PTC confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. Attackers are dropping JSP webshells on unpatched instances, so any manufacturing, engineering, or retail organization running these PLM systems should apply PTC's June 18 patch and check for the published indicators of compromise.

title: Nissan says Oracle PeopleSoft break-in may have spilled payroll records, SSNs
blurb: Nissan disclosed that an intrusion into its Oracle PeopleSoft system may have exposed employee payroll records and Social Security numbers, marking another breach tied to enterprise HR and ERP platforms. The incident pressures Oracle PeopleSoft customers to audit their on-prem deployments and patch cadence, since legacy ERP systems holding sensitive personnel data remain high-value targets even as vendors push migrations to managed cloud.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, and macOS 26.5.2 with patches for nearly 30 security vulnerabilities, most affecting WebKit and WebRTC along with several kernel-level fixes. The updates ship no new features and are recommended for all users, pulling fixes from the iOS 26.6 betas forward ahead of that release's public launch.

title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
blurb: Mustang Panda, a China-aligned espionage group, is running two campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets that abuse Zoho WorkDrive, a cloud storage platform common in India's public sector, as a covert command-and-control channel where malware reads commands and exfiltrates data through an attacker-controlled account. Acronis, which found active compromises on machines used by senior administrative staff and notified CERT-In, named three new tools and attributes the activity to the group with high confidence.

title: The Bear Necessities: A Look at the Drivers, Dynamics, and Applications of the Pro-Russia Influence Ecosystem
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group reported that the pro-Russia influence ecosystem is pivoting from Ukraine-focused operations back toward broader targeting of the EU, NATO, and other strategic priorities, with growing use of generative AI for planning, research, and content creation. The report warns that the ecosystem's interconnected structure makes it resilient to narrow takedowns, so enterprise security teams should plan for coordinated disruption rather than single-point mitigations.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: A CIO.com analysis argues enterprise AI cost overruns come not from senior staff running high-token sessions but from quiet, high-frequency automated background tasks, citing one firm that spent $500M in a single month on Claude after rolling it out without usage limits. The author models total cost as tokens × frequency × users and urges teams to size token volume per workflow before finalizing architecture, the step that separated budget-surprise deployments from controlled ones.

title: SCOTUS limits the law enforcement use of "geofence" warrants, saying people have "a reasonable expectation of privacy" in their cell-phone location data (TechCrunch)
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled that geofence warrants, which compel providers to identify every device in a geographic area during a time window, violate the Fourth Amendment because cell-phone location data carries a reasonable expectation of privacy. The decision constrains a fast-growing law enforcement technique and pressures Google, which fielded the bulk of these requests, and other location-data holders to tighten how they respond to government demands.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that government collection of phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending the privacy protections established in Carpenter v. United States to geofence-style data demands. The decision raises the legal bar for law enforcement requests to providers like Google and signals that enterprises holding customer location data will face tighter constraints on what they can hand over without a warrant.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture told some staff to stop using AI for basic tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after leaked internal meeting audio revealed "rapid escalation in AI token spend" that has executives questioning the return on their AI investment. The consultancy's agentic AI strategy lead framed unpredictable token costs as an industry-wide inflection point, one Accenture now plans to sell client services around as providers like Anthropic and GitHub shift to consumption-based pricing.

title: Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027
blurb: Microsoft extended hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, a year past the original October 2026 cutoff, with the existing patch cadence unchanged. The extension applies only to Azure Edition systems enrolled in hotpatching, which receive monthly security updates without reboots, keeping those workloads on a no-restart servicing path for an additional year.

title: Musk approved to acquire optical transceiver developer taking on Chinese dominance
blurb: Elon Musk received FTC fast-track antitrust approval to acquire Mesh Optical Technologies, a SpaceX-engineer-founded startup whose Alpha C1 transceiver moves 1.6 Tb/s at one-third the power of rivals by dropping retimer DSP silicon. The deal aims to challenge Chinese dominance in optical interconnects and secure a domestic, mass-producible supply for the AI clusters and Starlink-style space infrastructure across Musk's holdings.

title: Google Cloud to Offer Specialist AI Models for Science Research
blurb: Google Cloud will offer SandboxAQ's specialist AI models for drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor manufacturing through its cloud platform. The deal gives enterprise and research customers access to domain-specific scientific models without a separate vendor relationship, while extending SandboxAQ's distribution to Google Cloud's existing install base.

title: Microsoft Edge gets tons of security features, including AI model that can see your screen
blurb: Microsoft detailed new security features for Edge for Business, including shadow-AI controls that redirect employees to sanctioned tools like Microsoft 365 Copilot via Purview DLP, contractor file-download restrictions, and an on-device computer-vision model that watches the screen to block scareware regardless of site reputation. The features lean on native Defender and Purview integration, deepening the case for Edge in Entra-standardized shops while adding PAYG licensing costs for capabilities like shadow-AI redirection.

title: Microsoft to assist European Commission in defense of EU-US data-sharing agreement
blurb: Microsoft agreed to support the European Commission's legal defense of the Data Privacy Framework, the EU-US data-transfer agreement facing challenges in European courts. For enterprises running Microsoft cloud workloads across both regions, the vendor's backing of the framework matters because a court strike-down would force a scramble to re-paper transfer mechanisms and revisit data-residency commitments.

title: FCC escalates China crackdown by closing equipment import loophole
blurb: The FCC moved to bar pre-2022-authorized network equipment from Covered List vendors like Huawei and ZTE, closing a loophole that let barred gear remain in U.S. critical infrastructure and public-safety systems. The rules take effect in July and extend Chairman Brendan Carr's China crackdown, which already covers blanket carrier authorizations, foreign-made routers, and subsea cable provisions.

title: What the post-quantum executive order really demands of CISOs
blurb: The author argues the new post-quantum executive order reframes PQC as a readiness and ownership problem for CISOs, not a future cryptography exercise. Federal high-value systems must move key establishment to PQC by Dec. 31, 2030 and digital signatures by Dec. 31, 2031, deadlines that already sit inside enterprise procurement and architecture-review horizons. The more urgent risk is "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection, which means long-lived sensitive data is functionally exposed today regardless of the transition timeline.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy NAND and DRAM from blacklisted Chinese suppliers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The bid faces long odds, since acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio co-signed a 2022 Senate letter opposing the same YMTC sourcing on supply-chain security grounds, leaving Apple's component diversification strategy exposed to federal-use bans.

title: BT and Verizon spin off international networking arms into $4B joint venture
blurb: BT and Verizon are spinning off their international networking arms into a $4B joint venture combining global enterprise connectivity, managed network, and security services. The combined entity gives multinational customers a single provider for cross-border WAN and managed services, and consolidates two enterprise vendor relationships into one for IT buyers already running both carriers.

title: Visual Studio Code locks down untrusted code
blurb: Microsoft released VS Code 1.126, which now opens unfamiliar project folders in Restricted Mode under a new Workspace Trust feature that blocks automatic code execution until a developer explicitly trusts the folder. The release also surfaces total cost across an entire chat session rather than per-turn and lets a single Copilot agent host run multiple side-by-side chats within one shared working context.

title: Huawei autonomous networks framework canonized for telco agent proliferation
blurb: LF Networking formalized OpenAN, an open-source autonomous-networks framework first developed by Huawei and China Mobile, with Orange, ZTE, AIS, Infosys, and AsiaInfo on board. The project builds on Huawei's A2A-T protocol, which extends Google's A2A standard for telecom-specific multi-agent collaboration across OSS and EMS layers, targeting TM Forum Level 5 autonomy with scenario solution packages due at December's TM Forum event.

title: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron sued over alleged DRAM price fixing amid record memory costs — lawsuit claims coordinated HBM shift was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 production
blurb: Three plaintiffs filed a California class action on June 25th accusing Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron, which together control roughly 90% of the DRAM market, of coordinating to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 output and inflate prices the complaint says rose about 700% over four years. The suit invokes Section 1 of the Sherman Act and frames the industry-wide pivot to high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators as cover for cutting commodity supply, seeking treble damages and an injunction.

title: Meta is reusing old DDR4 RAM in its servers instead of buying new hardware
blurb: Meta built a custom ASIC called Vistara that lets it reuse DDR4 memory from decommissioned servers, bridging the old sticks to newer processors over CXL 2.0 across PCIe Gen5 so retired RAM keeps working in DDR5-based MemServers. The approach closes the gap between Meta's three-to-five-year server refresh cycle and seven-to-ten-year memory lifespan, promising meaningful savings as the company scales the architecture across millions of servers in its AI datacenters.

title: US seizes hundreds of FIFA World Cup illegal streaming domains
blurb: The US Justice Department seized nearly 400 domains streaming 2026 FIFA World Cup matches illegally, in a coordinated takedown dubbed Operation Offsides spanning Peru, Bulgaria, Croatia, Romania, Poland, and Colombia. The action drew leads from FIFA, NBCUniversal, beIN Media Group, and Warner Bros., and follows a separate shutdown of 44 PirloTV-linked piracy domains that drew over 950 million visits a year.

title: AI sprawl, token consumption ratchet up tech overspending
blurb: A Flexera survey of 500+ IT asset managers found more than two-thirds lack accurate visibility into AI software usage, and roughly 3 in 5 reported year-over-year AI overspend as wasted SaaS spend climbed 10 percentage points. The report names "tokenmaxxing," the practice of rewarding consumption without governing value, as a driver, citing Uber's burn through its entire 2026 AI budget in four months.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron Sued in US over Memory Price Fixing
blurb: Fourteen consumers and three small businesses sued Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron in California federal court, alleging the DRAM oligopoly colluded since 2022 to cut supply and drive prices up roughly 700% over four years. The plaintiffs claim the trio coordinated the shift to HBM and the discontinuation of DDR3 and DDR4, and if certified as a class action, the case could expose all three to triple damages.

title: FCC concludes AWS-3 spectrum, raises $3.5B
blurb: The FCC concluded Auction 113, raising over $3.5B from 17 bidders for 200 mid-band AWS-3 licenses that sat idle since EchoStar's Dish Network defaulted on them in 2014. Up to $3.3B of the proceeds will fund the FCC's "rip and replace" program to strip Chinese-made gear from US networks, with an Upper C-band auction of at least 100 MHz due by July 2027.

title: AWS raises GPU Capacity Block prices again as AI demand outstrips supply
blurb: AWS is raising prices for its EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML GPU reservation service by roughly 20% on July 1, the second increase this year after a 15% hike in January. An H200-based p5e.48xlarge instance that cost $34.61 per hour before January will run $39 in most regions and $49.74 in US West, as hyperscalers lock up Nvidia supply and squeeze enterprise buyers.

title: Gemini can now take notes in Google Meet for Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers.
blurb: Google added a "Take notes for me" feature to Google Meet for AI Pro and Ultra subscribers, with Gemini transcribing calls, generating summaries with action items, and saving them to a Google Doc plus an emailed recap. The feature is host-controlled and also extends to eligible Workspace business customers, notifying all participants when it is enabled.

title: 'Chinese Mythos' adds further fuel to sovereignty debate in AI security
blurb: Qihoo 360, a Chinese firm on the U.S. Entity List, released Yitianzhen and Tulongfeng, vulnerability-research models its CEO framed as China's sovereign answer to Anthropic's Mythos. The pitch leans on AI sovereignty rather than raw capability, with founder Hongyi Zhou conceding Chinese models trail U.S. frontier systems by 20% to 30% and claiming 3,432 flaws found, a figure Reuters notes is unverified.

title: Updated admin setting for improved video quality in Google Meet
blurb: Google updated the "Limit video bandwidth" admin setting in Google Meet so it now caps both downlink and uplink traffic rather than uplink only, while raising uplink usage for two-person calls to improve quality. Existing configurations carry over automatically with no end-user setting, and the change is rolling out to all Workspace customers starting June 29, 2026.

title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories
blurb: NVIDIA published its Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design, which moves agent execution off the employee's laptop, browser, or IDE and into a managed VM where identity, credentials, network access, runtime policy, and audit are enforced centrally. The argument for IT leaders is that always-on agents touching internal systems need the same governed perimeter as any privileged workload, with the endpoint reduced to a presentation layer and the VM as the isolation boundary.

title: Verizon and BT intertwine international enterprise efforts
blurb: Verizon and BT Group agreed to merge BT International and Verizon's international enterprise wireline arm into a 50:50 joint venture, with Verizon paying a $625M equalization payment reflecting BT's stronger contribution. The UK-headquartered JV serves over 3,000 customers across 180-plus countries on roughly $4B in combined annual revenue, pitching AI-ready, sovereign connectivity to multinationals and consolidating both carriers' cross-border enterprise operations under one organization led by new CEO Martijn Blanken.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup, Agentic CX designer for Amazon Connect Customer, EC2 AMI Watermarks, Open Governance for MySQL, and more (June 29, 2026)
blurb: AWS shipped its weekly roundup, headlined by Amazon Connect's no-code Agentic CX designer in preview and Lambda MicroVMs for isolated AI-code execution.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This CNCF post explains how to combine Linkerd's mesh-derived metrics with an existing OpenTelemetry pipeline, covering the east-west service traffic that application-layer instrumentation misses. Once a workload is meshed, the Linkerd proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request on port 4191, with no SDK calls or image rebuilds. The reference shows where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, plus a working Collector config and Grafana dashboard to land both layers in the same backend.

title: etcd-operator joins Cozystack with a new v1alpha2 API
blurb: The etcd-operator project was donated to Cozystack and re-implemented from scratch under a new etcd-operator.cozystack.io/v1alpha2 API, dropping the StatefulSet model in favor of driving etcd's native Membership API for MemberAdd, MemberPromote, and MemberRemove directly. The rewrite reconciles each member's Pod and PVC independently and adds learner-mode members one at a time, giving the operator full control over quorum changes during scaling and graceful removal.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell that lets servers and edge devices serve browser-based apps over SSH, with each app running as a dependency-free HTTP server bound to a Unix domain socket rather than a localhost port. Encryption is handled at the SSH layer instead of per-app, and apps register capabilities so they can discover each other, turning a remote box into a navigable desktop without a local GUI.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: The Git project released Git 2.55.0, adding a `git history fixup` command that amends staged changes into an existing commit and automatically rebases all stacked branches containing it. The release also brings the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without requiring a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: Zuck saves Meta bucks by reusing memory from old servers with a custom CXL ASIC
blurb: Meta built a custom CXL ASIC that pools DRAM salvaged from decommissioned servers, letting newer hosts borrow that memory over the CXL fabric instead of buying fresh DIMMs. The move targets memory cost and e-waste at hyperscale, extending the useful life of RAM that would otherwise be scrapped and easing capacity pressure on memory-bound workloads like caching and inference.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" shipped with systemd-based stage 1 initrd as the default, deprecating the old scripted implementation ahead of its removal in 26.11. The release lands 20,442 new packages and ships GCC 15 and GNOME 50, while marking the final Nixpkgs release to support x86_64-darwin before Apple-driven deprecation ends those builds at the close of 2026.

title: What is AI search optimization? (& why marketers should care)
blurb: AI search optimization, which HubSpot calls answer engine optimization, is the practice of making a brand more likely to be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and AI Overviews. The traffic is still small but converts well above search: across 1,200-plus publisher sites in a Microsoft Clarity study, AI-referred visitors signed up at roughly 11 times the rate of search visitors, and AI traffic grew 66% in 2025.

title: Trump threatens tariffs over EU digital taxes
blurb: Trump threatened 100% tariffs on any country that imposes a digital services tax on a U.S. company, escalating a long-running fight over how the EU and others tax American tech platforms. Marketers running cross-border campaigns on Google, Meta, and other U.S.-owned platforms should expect pricing and ad-policy turbulence if the threat translates into retaliatory levies or new DST regimes abroad.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: Shoppers arriving from AI assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini were 40% more likely to buy during Prime Day than those coming from search, email, or social, per Adobe, even as agentic AI still drives under 1% of retail traffic. Amazon is fighting that shift, suing Perplexity and blocking ChatGPT crawlers to keep shoppers on Rufus while quietly buying ChatGPT ads to pull them back.

title: OLIPOP’s Steven Vigilante Says Brands Are Misreading Creator Marketing: ‘One Video’ Is Not a Test
blurb: OLIPOP media lead Steven Vigilante told VidCon 2026 that judging a creator channel on a single video is a broken test, arguing brands should commit $25K to $50K per channel over one to three months before deciding whether it works. The framework helped grow OLIPOP past $400M in annual sales, and the brand now spends more on YouTube than Instagram. Vigilante's premise is that creators who actually use the product integrate it into content people choose to watch, outperforming interruptive ad formats.

title: Voters Think A.I. Is Terrible. In Campaigns, It’s Everywhere.
blurb: Political campaigns are deploying AI across the stack despite deep voter distrust, using it to generate campaign imagery, analyze voter data, and write custom voter messages at scale. The gap between public sentiment and operational reality is a warning for any brand marketer counting on AI for personalization, since the same tools that cut production cost can erode trust the moment audiences detect the machine behind the message.

title: Instagram is incorporating users' photos in ads for Meta Glasses
blurb: Meta is testing ads for its smart glasses that pull users' own Instagram photos directly into the creative, personalizing each placement with images the viewer already posted. The move signals where Meta is taking ad personalization, pushing marketers to weigh how dynamic, user-content-driven creative performs on CTR and brand trust before opting into formats that blur the line between organic posts and paid placements.

title: WhatsApp Opens Username Reservations to 3 Billion Users
blurb: WhatsApp opened username reservations to its 3 billion users, letting people connect without exchanging phone numbers.
